one_line_takeaway: On IoT-23, tree-based models (Random Forest, LightGBM) substantially outperform Logistic Regression and MLP for both binary and multiclass IoT malware classification and are more data-efficient, but all four models -- especially under multiclass settings with severe class imbalance -- suffer marked, uneven performance degradation over time (concept drift), underscoring a need for adaptive/continual retraining rather than static supervised deployment.
key_finding: Binary classification (Table V): RF and LGBM both exceed F1=0.998 (RF 0.9981, LGBM 0.9982), MLP reaches 0.9964, and LR is lowest but still solid at F1=0.9799. Multiclass classification (Table VI) shows much larger spread: RF F1=0.9771 (best), LGBM F1=0.9421 despite near-identical accuracy to RF (99.82%), MLP F1=0.8531 (high precision 0.9368 but lower recall 0.8508), and LR worst at F1=0.6571 despite 92.07% accuracy -- illustrating that accuracy is misleading under the dataset's severe class imbalance (e.g., C&C=8,685 and FileDownload=3 samples vs. Benign=8.78M). Training-size analysis (Tables III-IV) shows binary performance is stable and high even at 30% training data (RF F1=0.9987 at 30% training data, 0.9992 at 80%), while multiclass results are far more sensitive: RF's F1 jumps to 0.9797 at 50% training data (vs. 0.8372-0.8380 at 30-40%), and MLP's F1 rises steadily from 0.7227 (30%) to 0.8005 (80%). LGBM's multiclass F1 fluctuates non-monotonically (0.5648 at 30%, dropping to 0.4143 at 50%, recovering to 0.5723 at 80%) despite high raw accuracy, which the authors attribute to difficulty capturing minority-class patterns. Temporal (rolling-window) evaluation shows all models degrade over time as malware diversity increases in later months, with RF exhibiting comparatively more stable performance than the others across both binary and multiclass settings, though all models show sharp performance drops in specific months (e.g., several models hit F1 near 0.00 in later test months in the multiclass setting).
core_contribution: An empirical benchmarking study (not a novel algorithm) that systematically compares four supervised ML models -- Logistic Regression (LR), Random Forest (RF), LightGBM (LGBM), and a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) -- for IoT malware detection on the IoT-23 dataset, across three axes: (1) binary vs. multiclass classification performance under 5-fold cross-validation, (2) sensitivity to training-set size (30%-80% of data), and (3) temporal robustness/concept drift via a rolling-window evaluation that trains on earlier months and tests on later ones. Preprocessing pipeline: concatenation of per-capture CSVs, dropping UID/IP fields (and local_orig/local_resp, which had no recorded values), timestamp-derived hour-of-day/day-of-week features, categorical encoding, mutual-information-based feature selection (top 10 features reported in Table I, e.g., id.orig_p, id.resp_p, proto, duration, orig_bytes, conn_state, history, orig_pkts, orig_ip_bytes, resp_ip_bytes), and Min-Max scaling. Hyperparameters were tuned per model/task via RandomizedSearchCV (50 configurations) and reused across the training-size and temporal experiments.
threat_model: The paper does not propose or analyze an adversarial threat model (no adversarial ML, evasion, or poisoning attacks are considered). The 'threat' addressed is the practical deployment challenge of detecting and classifying real IoT malware traffic (C&C, DDoS, horizontal port scan, malicious file download, generic 'Attack') under realistic constraints: scarce labeled data and non-stationary/evolving malware distributions over time (concept drift). The adversary is implicit -- IoT botnet/malware operators reflected in the IoT-23 captures -- rather than an adaptive attacker targeting the ML pipeline itself.
limitations: The paper itself notes (in its own discussion) that all models exhibit performance degradation over time, motivating future work on adaptive/continual learning (fine-tuning, online learning, active learning) that this study does not implement or evaluate. Other limitations evident from the text itself: (1) only 25M of ~325M labeled flows are sampled due to hardware constraints, with no analysis of whether/how this subsampling affects class-imbalance severity or generalizability; (2) the temporal/rolling-window evaluation uses a fixed set of 8 sparse, non-contiguous test months (per Table VII, some months have very few or highly imbalanced malware families present, e.g., 2019-01 lacks 'Attack' and 'PortScan' entirely), which likely explains some of the extreme month-to-month F1 volatility (e.g., near-zero scores) rather than solely reflecting genuine concept drift; (3) hyperparameters tuned via cross-validation are reused unchanged for the training-size and temporal experiments rather than retuned, which the authors state is deliberate (to isolate the effect of data availability/drift) but limits interpretation of absolute performance under those conditions; (4) no adversarial robustness, feature-tampering, or evasion analysis is performed despite this being a security-relevant deployment context; (5) IoT-23 is a single dataset from 2018-2019, so generalization to other IoT environments/newer malware families is untested.

datasets_used: IoT-23 (Stratosphere Laboratory; 23 pcap captures, 20 malware-infected + 3 benign, 2018-2019; processed CSV version via Kaggle with 21 original features; ~325M labeled flows total; 25M flows sampled for experiments)
quantitative_results: Binary classification F1: LR 0.9799, RF 0.9981, LGBM 0.9982, MLP 0.9964 (Table V)
Binary classification Accuracy: LR 98.15%, RF 99.83%, LGBM 99.84%, MLP 99.67% (Table V)
Multiclass classification F1: LR 0.6571, RF 0.9771, LGBM 0.9421, MLP 0.8531 (Table VI)
Multiclass classification Accuracy: LR 92.07%, RF 99.81%, LGBM 99.82%, MLP 99.54% (Table VI)
Multiclass F1 at 30% vs 80% training data: LR 0.6125->0.6052, RF 0.8372->0.9769 (RF hits 0.9797 at 50%), LGBM 0.5648->0.5723 (non-monotonic, dips to 0.4143 at 50%), MLP 0.7227->0.8005 (Table IV)
Binary F1 at 30% vs 80% training data: LR 0.9853->0.9854, RF 0.9987->0.9992, LGBM 0.9987->0.9987 (stable), MLP 0.9977->0.9968 (Table III)
Sampled label distribution: Benign 8,780,158; Malicious 7,055,007; DDoS 5,778,154; PortScan 3,386,241; C&C 8,685; Attack 2,755; FileDownload 3 (Fig. 2)

Temporal evaluation across 8 test months (2018-07 through 2019-09): all models show F1 degradation and volatility in later months, more pronounced in multiclass than binary; RF shows relatively higher stability but still drops (e.g., F1 near 0 for RF in some later multiclass test months per Fig. 3b heatmap values)
baselines_compared: Multi-Layer Perceptron (2 hidden layers: 64, 128 neurons; ReLU; dropout 0.2)
